article_title: Buy Now, Pay Later Faces Its Regulatory Reckoning as UK Finalizes Rules and New York Proposes Nation's First Licensing Regime
summary: Strong analysis with one dead source URL requiring replacement before publication

content_preview.title: Buy Now, Pay Later Faces Its Regulatory Reckoning as UK Finalizes Rules and New York Proposes Nation's First Licensing Regime
content_preview.summary: The UK's FCA will enforce full oversight of BNPL products from July 15, while New York has proposed the first comprehensive state licensing framework — filling a gap left by the CFPB's withdrawal of federal consumer protections.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The buy now, pay later industry is entering its most consequential regulatory period since the first BNPL products appeared more than a decade ago. On February 11, the UK's Financial Conduct Authority published [final rules](https://www.reuters.com/business/finance/uk-fca-finalises-buy-now-pay-later-rules-2026-02-11) that will bring the sector under full regulatory oversight from July 15, 2026 — classifying BNPL as "Deferred Payment Credit" subject to creditworthiness checks, disclo...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt
1 source(s) returned dead link errors — verify URLs before resubmitting
2 source(s) blocked bot access — sources likely valid but not archivable automatically

editor_notes.overall_assessment: Excellent analysis of BNPL regulatory developments across UK, US federal, and NY state levels. The article is well-structured with clear section breaks and strong sourcing from government and research institutions. However, the FT source URL appears fabricated (FT uses UUID-based URLs, not descriptive slugs) and returns 404. The remaining 6 sources are verified and support the article's claims. NY governor press release confirmed. Reuters and Bloomberg are likely paywalled rather than dead.
concerns: The Financial Times URL (https://www.ft.com/content/buy-now-pay-later-fca-regulation-2026) returns HTTP 404 and appears to use a fabricated slug format inconsistent with FT's UUID-based URL scheme
The FT source is listed but not directly hyperlinked in the article body — if it supported specific claims, those claims need a replacement source
recommendations: Replace the dead FT URL with a working source that covers FCA BNPL regulation — the FCA's own website or a verifiable news article would suffice
Remove the Bloomberg source about Upvest fundraising if it is not directly relevant to the BNPL regulatory narrative
